page.title: State income tax and unemployment insurance — Minnesota

hero.headline: Minnesota payroll taxes: what you owe as an employer
hero.subhead: Four brackets, a $44,000 UI wage base, and new PFML from January 2026.
hero.partner_badge: Served by Teamed-owned entity: Teamed US Inc., Delaware

sections.question: How does Minnesota state income tax work for employers?
sections.answer_first: Minnesota has four income tax brackets running from 5.35% to 9.85%. You withhold at the employee's marginal rate using the 2026 tables published by the Minnesota Department of Revenue.
sections.body:
The 5.35% entry rate applies to the first $33,310 of income for single filers in 2026. The top marginal rate of 9.85% kicks in above $203,151 for singles and $337,931 for married filers — placing Minnesota among the ten highest top-rate states in the country. Brackets are recalculated each year against the US Chained CPI, rounded to the nearest $10.

For supplemental wages (bonuses, commissions, back pay), you withhold at a flat 6.25% rate rather than the employee's marginal bracket. Every employee hired in Minnesota must complete Form W-4MN.

Minnesota has no city or county income tax — you deal with one withholding account, not multiple local jurisdictions. File Form MW-1 monthly or quarterly depending on your total Minnesota withholding volume, with an annual reconciliation (Form W-3MN) due by 28 February.

Teamed's payroll specialists handle all Minnesota withholding filings. You see every item on the invoice.

data_table.caption: Minnesota income tax brackets — single filer, tax year 2026
data_table.source: Minnesota Department of Revenue · revenue.state.mn.us · Accessed 30 May 2026

sections.question: What is Minnesota's unemployment insurance rate in 2026?
sections.answer_first: Minnesota's UI taxable wage base is $44,000 in 2026. New employers in most non-construction industries pay a 1.00% rate; experienced employers range from 0.00% to 8.90% based on claims history.
sections.body:
The base tax rate for 2026 is 0.40%, but a 14% additional assessment applies when the UI Trust Fund falls below a statutory threshold — which it does in 2026. That multiplies the effective tax charge. Experience-rated employers receive their individual Rate Determination by post each December.

Construction is a different calculation. New construction employers start at rates as high as 8.90% (highway, masonry, roofing), reflecting the industry's historically higher claim frequency. If you're hiring a Minnesota-based software engineer or analyst, the 1.00% new-employer rate is the applicable starting point.

UI is an employer-only tax. You don't withhold anything from the employee's pay for this item. Quarterly filing through DEED's UIMN portal is required regardless of your company's home state. Teamed files and pays Minnesota UI on your behalf; the actual cost passes through at cost on your invoice — no markup.

Run the employer cost calculator to see the full Minnesota cost stack before you make an offer.

sections.question: What is Minnesota's Paid Family and Medical Leave law?
sections.answer_first: Minnesota PFML went live on 1 January 2026. The total premium is 0.88% of wages, split 50/50 between employer and employee by default, against a $185,000 wage base.
sections.body:
Employees can take up to 12 weeks of family leave and up to 12 weeks of medical leave in a benefit year, with a hard cap of 20 weeks combined. The maximum weekly benefit equals the state average weekly wage: $1,423 in 2026.

You must contribute at least 0.44% of covered wages (the employer's half). You may deduct up to 0.44% from employee pay to cover the employee's half. If your team has 30 or fewer employees and average wages are at or below 150% of the state average, you pay a reduced rate of 75% of the standard premium.

First premium payments for Q1 2026 wages were due by 30 April 2026. If you're setting up a new Minnesota hire today, factor this into your payroll configuration immediately — the obligation started at hire, not at your next quarterly filing.

Private plans are allowed if they meet or exceed state-plan benefits. Teamed's named HR specialists will flag which private-plan option (if any) fits your headcount and leave-policy philosophy. All premium calculations are visible on your invoice line-by-line.

sections.question: What sick leave must Minnesota employers provide?
sections.answer_first: Minnesota's Earned Sick and Safe Time (ESST) law requires all employers to provide paid sick leave. Employees accrue one hour for every 30 hours worked, up to 48 hours per year.
sections.body:
ESST has been in force since 1 January 2024 and applies to every Minnesota employer — there's no small-business carve-out. Accrued hours carry over year-to-year until the employee's balance reaches 80 hours.

From 1 January 2026, you may advance ESST hours to employees at the start of the year based on estimated accrual — a useful option for simplifying administration. If your advance falls short of what the employee actually accrues during the year, you must make up the difference.

ESST covers the employee's own illness, a family member's care needs, preventive care appointments, and safety-related absences (domestic abuse, stalking). Minneapolis and other cities had pre-existing sick time ordinances; ESST sets the state floor but city ordinances may be more generous.

Your Teamed contract automatically incorporates ESST tracking and accrual. It runs on the same platform as your other Minnesota compliance items.

sections.question: How does Minnesota define independent contractors?
sections.answer_first: Minnesota uses separate statutory tests for construction workers (14 factors under § 181.723) and general workers (§ 181.722). Misclassification carries penalties of up to $10,000 per worker.
sections.body:
For non-construction workers, Minnesota Rule 5200.0221 and Minn. Stat. § 181.722, subd. 3 set out the general misclassification test. The Minnesota Department of Revenue runs a separate test for income tax withholding purposes — two agencies, two tests, one worker.

For construction, § 181.723, subd. 4 (effective 1 March 2025) expanded the test from nine to 14 factors. The worker must operate as a registered business entity, own or lease their own equipment, invoice under a business name, and serve multiple clients — among other requirements. All factors must be met.

Penalties for misclassification include up to $10,000 per violation, $10,000 per misclassified employee, plus back wages, overtime, benefits, and health insurance contributions. The Minnesota Attorney General's office actively prosecutes construction-sector misclassification.

Teamed's contractor classification tool lets you test any Minnesota engagement before you sign a contract. A real human from our legal team reviews borderline cases.

sections.question: What does a Minnesota hire actually cost as an employer?
sections.answer_first: A new non-construction Minnesota hire carries roughly 9.7% of gross salary in mandatory employer-side taxes: FICA 7.65%, FUTA 0.60%, UI 1.00%, and PFML 0.44%.
sections.body:
Add Teamed's flat rate of $599 per employee per month — the same price as Deel, every cost itemised on every invoice, Zero FX mark-up in any currency pairing.

The FICA split: you pay 6.2% toward Social Security (up to the $184,500 federal wage base in 2026) and 1.45% for Medicare with no cap. Both are matched dollar-for-dollar from your pocket — not the employee's.

UI sits on top of that: 1.00% on the first $44,000 for a new-employer non-construction hire. For higher earners, the $44,000 wage base means the absolute UI cost per employee maxes at $440 annually (at 1.00% rate), before the experience-rating adjustment.

PFML adds 0.44% of the employee's wages up to $185,000 — so up to $814 per year at the employer's minimum share.

The full cost picture goes into the employer cost calculator. Statutory amounts pass through at cost on your Teamed invoice — you see every line.

pricing_block.statutory_costs_note: Statutory employer taxes (FICA, UI, PFML) are passed through at cost and itemised separately on every invoice.

tagline_close.note: Minnesota's 9.85% top marginal rate doesn't show up on your payslip — it shows up in your retention conversations six months in. The PFML premium went live in January 2026 and most EOR invoices still don't show you how it's calculated. The construction IC test grew from nine factors to 14 in March 2025. These are the specifics that matter before you make an offer. EOR is the right model for Minnesota, until it isn't — when your headcount justifies a US entity, we'll run the numbers honestly and tell you.

faq_schema.q: What are Minnesota state income tax withholding rates for 2026?
faq_schema.a: Minnesota has four brackets: 5.35% on the first $33,310, 6.80% up to $109,430, 7.85% up to $203,150, and 9.85% above $203,151 for single filers. Supplemental wages are withheld at a flat 6.25%. Source: Minnesota Department of Revenue.

faq_schema.q: What is the Minnesota unemployment insurance taxable wage base in 2026?
faq_schema.a: The Minnesota UI taxable wage base is $44,000 per employee in 2026. New employers in most non-construction industries pay a 1.00% rate. Experience-rated employers range from 0.00% to 8.90%. UI is an employer-only tax. Source: UIMN (uimn.org).

faq_schema.q: Does Minnesota have paid family and medical leave in 2026?
faq_schema.a: Yes. Minnesota PFML launched on 1 January 2026. The total premium is 0.88% of wages (employer minimum 0.44%, employee maximum 0.44%) against a $185,000 wage base. Employees can take up to 12 weeks family leave and 12 weeks medical leave, capped at 20 combined weeks per year.

faq_schema.q: What sick leave is required for Minnesota employees?
faq_schema.a: Minnesota Earned Sick and Safe Time (ESST) requires all employers to provide one hour of paid sick leave per 30 hours worked, up to 48 hours per year with an 80-hour accrual cap. ESST has applied to all Minnesota employers since 1 January 2024.

faq_schema.q: What is the independent contractor test in Minnesota?
faq_schema.a: Minnesota uses Minn. Stat. § 181.722, subd. 3 for general workers and § 181.723, subd. 4 for construction. The construction test was expanded to 14 factors on 1 March 2025. Misclassification carries penalties of up to $10,000 per worker plus back wages and benefits.
